romania.title: Senatul a adoptat un proiect prin care fermierii pot cere demolarea construcțiilor ilegale și accesa fonduri PAC pentru modernizare
romania.summary: Senatul a adoptat, marți, un proiect de lege care le permite fermierilor poti iniția proceduri juridice de demolare a locuințelor construite ilegal în jurul fermelor, cu scopul prevenirii disconfortului și riscurilor sanitare și să acceseze fonduri europene, prin Planul strategic PAC 2023-2027, pentru modernizarea și restructurarea fermelor agricole.

romania.title: CAHM Europe scrie istorie la nivel global, compania românească câștigă premiul Green & Organic la „Oscarurile” industriei de beauty
romania.summary: Inovația românească redefinește produsele de igienă personală sustenabile Qpearl, premiată în cadrul celei de-a 57-a ediții Cosmoprof Worldwide Bologna 2026 București, 7 aprilie 2026 —Compania românească CAHM Europe a câștigat… Articolul CAHM Europe scrie istorie la nivel global, compania românească câștigă premiul Green & Organic la „Oscarurile” industriei de beauty apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: 😂 Realitatea Plus se închide. Călin Georgescu se întoarce pe Minimax
romania.summary: Milioane de copii între 3 şi 9 ani care urmăreau cu sufletul la gură peripeţiile lui Călin Georgescu în fiecare dimineaţă au izbucnit în plâns la auzul veştii că Realitatea Plus şi-a pierdut licenţa de emisie. Din fericire pentru ei, aventura nu se încheie aici! Într-o conferinţă de presă, îndrăgitul spiriduş simpatic Georgescu a anunţat […] The post Realitatea Plus se închide. Călin Georgescu se întoarce pe Minimax appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Fulgy, fiul Clejanilor, a fost internat la Obregia după ce a amenințat că aruncă un bloc în aer. Au intervenit și negociatorii SAS
romania.summary: Polițiștii au intervenit în cursul serii de luni, 6 aprilie, la un bloc din Sectorul 3, după ce un bărbat a amenințat că a lăsat gazele pornite în locuință și că urmează să arunce blocul în aer. Potrivit surselor Digi24.ro, este vorba de Fulgy, fiul artiștilor Viorica și Ionică de la Clejani, care într-un final a fost internat la Spitalul Clinic de Psihiatrie „Dr. Alexandru Obregia”.

romania.title: FORME: un nou spațiu dedicat designului românesc contemporan s-a deschis în centrul Bucureștiului
romania.summary: FORME, un concept colaborativ inedit inițiat de IUTTA a fost inaugurat în cadrul Sheraton Hotel din capitală, oferind o abordare diferită asupra retailului: un spațiu în care obiectele nu sunt… Articolul FORME: un nou spațiu dedicat designului românesc contemporan s-a deschis în centrul Bucureștiului apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

Povestea proiectului „Leadership HUB” începe cu un proiect finanțat de Teach for Romania și continuă astăzi prin colaborări internaționale în cadrul platformei eTwinning și al […]

romania.title: Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil
romania.summary: Află povestea lui Ionuț și cum sprijinul educațional și emoțional oferit de Fundația Inocenți ajută copiii vulnerabili să își descopere încrederea în sine. The post Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

romania.title: Identități 2026: debutul artistic al noii generații de bursieri ai programului Tinere Talente
romania.summary: 36 de tineri artiști vizuali și muzicieni, susținuți de către Fundația Regală Margareta a României, se prezintă publicului pe 2 martie, la Elite Art Gallery, București Fundația Regală Margareta a României anunță Identități, evenimentul care marchează debutul public al generației 2026 a programului național... Post-ul Identități 2026: debutul artistic al noii generații de bursieri ai programului Tinere Talente apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

medical.title: Blood-based biomarker could help stratify cancer risk in Lynch Syndrome
medical.summary: Researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center have discovered a new blood-based biomarker that can help identify and characterize asymptomatic people with Lynch Syndrome (LS) who are more susceptible to developing cancer based on early immune detection signatures, allowing clinicians to stratify patients based on their personal risk level.

medical.title: While health care struggled to keep up during COVID-19, community clinics never wavered
medical.summary: As the COVID-19 pandemic upended virtually every aspect of people's lives, one critical question loomed large for millions of low-income and underserved health care patients: Would they still be able to see their own doctor? Researchers at Case Western Reserve University and the OCHIN nonprofit consultancy conducted a comprehensive, nationwide study of community-based health centers—the frontline primary care providers serving millions of America's most vulnerable patients. The goal: to assess how consistently patients were able to see their primary care provider before, during, and after the COVID-19 pandemic.

medical.title: Scientists uncover previously unknown chromatin regulation mechanism with therapeutic potential for pediatric cancer
medical.summary: As many as 1 in 4 cancers are driven by mutations in the SWI/SNF chromatin-remodeling complex, which controls access to DNA. A study led by St. Jude Children's Research Hospital recently identified the gene-regulatory protein PHIP as a critical vulnerability in cancers driven by broad SWI/SNF inactivation. The work revealed PHIP as a potential therapeutic target for cancers, including rhabdoid tumors in children, and other hard-to-treat malignancies. The results are published in Nature Communications.

medical.title: AI-driven tests can turn patient feedback into better vision care
medical.summary: In eye clinics and clinical trials, doctors increasingly rely on patients' own reports of how eye disease and treatment affect daily life. Yet these patient questionnaires are often long, repetitive and difficult to use in real-time care, limiting their value in both research and care improvement.

medical.title: Alzheimer's screening tools may work differently for women and men
medical.summary: A Georgia State University study shows standard cognitive screening tools used to monitor Alzheimer's disease may not reflect underlying brain changes in the same way for women and men. According to the Alzheimer's Association, nearly two-thirds of Americans living with Alzheimer's are women. New Georgia State research published in the journal Brain Communications adds to growing evidence that Alzheimer's may progress differently in men and women—and that those differences could matter in clinical care. It also suggests doctors may need to interpret common tests differently for each sex.

medical.title: Massive eye drop recall reflects ongoing issues with manufacturing and FDA inspection
medical.summary: A California company has recalled more than 3.1 million bottles of lubricating eye drops because it had not properly tested—and thus could not prove—whether the products were sterile. These products are sold under several names at major retailers across the country. The company, K.C. Pharmaceuticals, initiated the recall on March 3, 2026.

medical.title: A better way to see how brain cells falter in disease
medical.summary: To gain better insight into what's happening in the brain, researchers examine the molecules produced by brain cells, including RNA and proteins. But existing methods for molecular profiling don't always capture the cells' full complexity.

medical.title: A woman's 'push‑up hack' is trending on social media. An anatomist explains why it works
medical.summary: Push-ups can be a challenge for the best of us. But many women struggle to do them, even after months of training in the gym.

medical.title: AI tool can predict Barrett's esophagus recurrence after therapy with 90% accuracy
medical.summary: A new artificial intelligence (AI)-based tool shows promise for improving surveillance in patients treated with endoscopic eradication therapies for Barrett's esophagus (BE)-related dysplasia and early esophageal adenocarcinoma. BE is the only known condition that precedes esophageal adenocarcinoma—an aggressive cancer with high mortality rates.

medical.title: Cutting emissions is not the only way to save lives from air pollution
medical.summary: A major new global modeling study led by researchers at Stockholm Environment Institute (SEI) at the University of York challenges the international focus on air pollution. Published in The Lancet Planetary Health, the study finds that reducing population vulnerability is as important as cutting emissions for saving lives.

medical.title: Study finds rising rates of electric scooter injuries, with racial and ethnic disparities among those harmed
medical.summary: As electric scooters (e-scooters) become more available in cities and urban areas, injuries are becoming more common—especially among teenage boys. This is among the findings from a new study using a national database of e-scooter injuries, which also found racial and ethnic disparities among those injured.

science.title: Network analysis reveals mammal food web drivers across Africa
science.summary: Ecology is often understood as a hyperlocal thing. The ecology of a pond, for instance, is vastly complex, even if the pond is tiny. But learning solely from local ecosystems is a slow and laborious approach that may not capture widely applicable findings.

science.title: From decades-long studies of humble grasses, new clues to climate resistance
science.summary: In parts of the Midwest and Great Plains, feathery yellow goldenrod and stands of big bluestem sway alongside Indiangrass and other prairie plants, stretching up to eight feet tall. Now, in the search for ways to help ecosystems withstand the weather extremes made worse by climate change, it seems that humble grassland plants like these may have some of the answers.

science.title: Ranks of Disparity: New approach fixes flaw in fairness algorithms
science.summary: As organizations increasingly rely on algorithms to rank candidates for jobs, university spots, and financial services, a new method, named hyperFA*IR, offers a more principled approach when picking candidates based on a limited pool of applicants, especially if minorities are few. The new interactive visualization, "Ranks of Disparity," makes these complex dynamics visible.

science.title: Students prefer AI chatbots, until they know it is one
science.summary: Do chatbots have a role in higher education? It's a question Joshua Lambert, an associate professor and biostatistician in the University of Cincinnati College of Nursing, is pondering. He's turned to a group of his students to find out their thoughts about the helpfulness and satisfaction of a custom AI education chatbot.

science.title: Why AI shouldn't be used even to decide 'simple' court cases
science.summary: In just a few years, generative artificial intelligence (gen AI) has brought about significant changes in many industries, from health care to education, entertainment to finance, and even law.

science.title: More than a pretty picture, star-shaped nanomaterial changes energy storage
science.summary: When created at the nanoscale, materials can resemble shapes like stars, rods or even pyramids. These particle shapes, also known as the morphologies of a solid, make for more than just interesting images under a microscope—they can determine how the material behaves, sometimes in dramatic ways. University at Buffalo researchers have demonstrated this phenomenon by creating the first-ever star-shaped vanadyl hydroxide (VOOH) and shown that this shape can fundamentally alter how the material stores energy.

science.title: Early humans in South Africa were quarrying stone as far back as 220,000 years ago
science.summary: As long as 220,000 years ago—far earlier than previously thought—people quarried rocks for their tools in places they specifically sought out. An international research team led by the University of Tübingen has demonstrated this behavior at the Jojosi site in South Africa, challenging the prevailing view that Paleolithic hunter–gatherers collected their raw materials incidentally during other activities. The study is published in the journal Nature Communications.

science.title: New leading cause of tree death in US northeast shifts from logging to natural causes
science.summary: Why do trees fall in the forest? The Answer is Changing. New research suggests that in just 15 years, the causes of most tree loss have flipped from human hands to a handful of natural causes.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Senatul a adoptat un proiect prin care fermierii pot cere demolarea construcțiilor ilegale și accesa fonduri PAC pentru modernizare
mix_items.summary: Senatul a adoptat, marți, un proiect de lege care le permite fermierilor poti iniția proceduri juridice de demolare a locuințelor construite ilegal în jurul fermelor, cu scopul prevenirii disconfortului și riscurilor sanitare și să acceseze fonduri europene, prin Planul strategic PAC 2023-2027, pentru modernizarea și restructurarea fermelor agricole.

He's turned to a group of his students to find out their thoughts about the helpfulness and satisfaction of a custom AI education chatbot.

mix_items.title: AI-driven tests can turn patient feedback into better vision care
mix_items.summary: In eye clinics and clinical trials, doctors increasingly rely on patients' own reports of how eye disease and treatment affect daily life. Yet these patient questionnaires are often long, repetitive and difficult to use in real-time care, limiting their value in both research and care improvement.

mix_items.title: Why AI shouldn't be used even to decide 'simple' court cases
mix_items.summary: In just a few years, generative artificial intelligence (gen AI) has brought about significant changes in many industries, from health care to education, entertainment to finance, and even law.

mix_items.title: Alzheimer's screening tools may work differently for women and men
mix_items.summary: A Georgia State University study shows standard cognitive screening tools used to monitor Alzheimer's disease may not reflect underlying brain changes in the same way for women and men. According to the Alzheimer's Association, nearly two-thirds of Americans living with Alzheimer's are women. New Georgia State research published in the journal Brain Communications adds to growing evidence that Alzheimer's may progress differently in men and women—and that those differences could matter in clinical care. It also suggests doctors may need to interpret common tests differently for each sex.

mix_items.title: More than a pretty picture, star-shaped nanomaterial changes energy storage
mix_items.summary: When created at the nanoscale, materials can resemble shapes like stars, rods or even pyramids. These particle shapes, also known as the morphologies of a solid, make for more than just interesting images under a microscope—they can determine how the material behaves, sometimes in dramatic ways. University at Buffalo researchers have demonstrated this phenomenon by creating the first-ever star-shaped vanadyl hydroxide (VOOH) and shown that this shape can fundamentally alter how the material stores energy.

mix_items.title: Massive eye drop recall reflects ongoing issues with manufacturing and FDA inspection
mix_items.summary: A California company has recalled more than 3.1 million bottles of lubricating eye drops because it had not properly tested—and thus could not prove—whether the products were sterile. These products are sold under several names at major retailers across the country. The company, K.C. Pharmaceuticals, initiated the recall on March 3, 2026.

mix_items.title: A better way to see how brain cells falter in disease
mix_items.summary: To gain better insight into what's happening in the brain, researchers examine the molecules produced by brain cells, including RNA and proteins. But existing methods for molecular profiling don't always capture the cells' full complexity.

mix_items.title: A woman's 'push‑up hack' is trending on social media. An anatomist explains why it works
mix_items.summary: Push-ups can be a challenge for the best of us. But many women struggle to do them, even after months of training in the gym.

mix_items.title: AI tool can predict Barrett's esophagus recurrence after therapy with 90% accuracy
mix_items.summary: A new artificial intelligence (AI)-based tool shows promise for improving surveillance in patients treated with endoscopic eradication therapies for Barrett's esophagus (BE)-related dysplasia and early esophageal adenocarcinoma. BE is the only known condition that precedes esophageal adenocarcinoma—an aggressive cancer with high mortality rates.

mix_items.title: Early humans in South Africa were quarrying stone as far back as 220,000 years ago
mix_items.summary: As long as 220,000 years ago—far earlier than previously thought—people quarried rocks for their tools in places they specifically sought out. An international research team led by the University of Tübingen has demonstrated this behavior at the Jojosi site in South Africa, challenging the prevailing view that Paleolithic hunter–gatherers collected their raw materials incidentally during other activities. The study is published in the journal Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: Cutting emissions is not the only way to save lives from air pollution
mix_items.summary: A major new global modeling study led by researchers at Stockholm Environment Institute (SEI) at the University of York challenges the international focus on air pollution. Published in The Lancet Planetary Health, the study finds that reducing population vulnerability is as important as cutting emissions for saving lives.

mix_items.title: New leading cause of tree death in US northeast shifts from logging to natural causes
mix_items.summary: Why do trees fall in the forest? The Answer is Changing. New research suggests that in just 15 years, the causes of most tree loss have flipped from human hands to a handful of natural causes.

mix_items.title: Study finds rising rates of electric scooter injuries, with racial and ethnic disparities among those harmed
mix_items.summary: As electric scooters (e-scooters) become more available in cities and urban areas, injuries are becoming more common—especially among teenage boys. This is among the findings from a new study using a national database of e-scooter injuries, which also found racial and ethnic disparities among those injured.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: M-am apucat de citit. Dupa 5 pagini am luat pauza de reflectie de 2 ore.

satire_ro.title: Precizare NASA: „Așa-zisul borcan cu Nutella de pe Artemis II nu era cu Nutella”
